documents:
Course: AFRAM 101 - Introduction to African American Studies
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: History, culture, religion, institutions, politics, economics, arts, and psychology of peoples of African descent as developed from experience in both the old and new worlds. Multidisciplinary analysis of social life from a black perspective as illustrated in selected historical and contemporary writings.

Course: AFRAM 102 - Advanced Placement
Credits: AP
General Education: Introduction to African American Studies (5) SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Course awarded based on Advanced Placement (AP) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information.

Course: AFRAM 150 - Introduction to African American History
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introductory survey of topics and problems in African American history with some attention to Africa as well as to America. Basic introductory course for sequence of lecture courses and seminars in African American history. Offered: jointly with HSTAA 150.

Course: AFRAM 214 - Introduction African American Literature
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introduction to various genres of African American literature from its beginnings to the present. Emphasizes the cultural and historical context of African American literary expression and its aesthetics criteria. Explores key issues and debates, such as race and racism, inequality, literary form, and canonical acceptance. Offered: jointly with ENGL 258.

Course: AFRAM 220 - African American Film Studies
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H/SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Examines the history and theory of African American filmmaking, introducing central political and aesthetic debates by way of different cinematic eras, genres, and filmmakers. Focuses primarily on black directors and producers independent and commercial contexts as they confront popular representations of U.S. blackness in their own cinematic practice.

Course: AFRAM 241 - The United States During the Era of Civil War and Reconstruction
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Views American Civil War and Reconstruction through lens of African-American social history. From U.S. government's abolition of Atlantic slave trade in 1808 to expanding Jim Crow laws in 1890s, reports how changes in the era were racialized and gendered. Considers how ideas of race, gender, class, and freedom in the U.S. were challenged and reshaped. Explores historical legacies of Civil War and Reconstruction from the nineteenth-century to now. Offered: jointly with HSTAA 241.

Course: AFRAM 246 - African American Politics
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Survey of African Americans within the U.S. socio-political processes. Situates African Americans within a post-civil rights context where there is debate about race's centrality to an African American politics. Offered: jointly with POL S 246.

Course: AFRAM 260 - African American Family
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Explores cultural processes as well as political, economic, and social structures and policies that have shaped, and continue to shape, the ways in which African American families have formed and functioned. Examines the history, experiences, struggles, and progress of African American families in the U.S. beginning with pre-Colonial Africa and ending with current issues facing African American families. Offered: jointly with SOC 260.

Course: AFRAM 261 - The African American Experience through Literature
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H/SSc
Prerequisites: None
Description: Instructs students in hermeneutical and sociological methods of analyses. Analyzes selected novels, essays, poems, short stories, and plays with the purpose of understanding the structures and functions of both society and personality. Offered: jointly with SOC 261.

Course: AFRAM 270 - The Jazz Age
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Interdisciplinary study of period after World War I to Great Crash. African American and Anglo American currents and impulses that flowed together in the Roaring Twenties. Covers politics of normalcy, economics of margin, literature of indulgence and confusion, transformation of race relations, and cultural influence of jazz. Offered: jointly with HSTAA 270.

Course: AFRAM 272 - History of the South Since the Civil War
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Reconstruction and its aftermath, the Agrarian (Populist) revolt, disfranchisement and segregation, the effects of urbanization and subsequent depression, desegregation, and the struggle for civil rights. Examines the New South, the conflict of ideology with structural and material change, and the place of the South in contemporary America.

Course: AFRAM 315 - Black Identities and Political Power
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc
Prerequisites: either AES 150, AFRAM 150, AFRAM 201, or POL S 201. Offered: jointly with POL S 315.
Description: Relates the deployment of political power within institutions to shifting racial identities. Shows how racial identities both reflect and inflect relations of domination and resistance within and between cultures in the black diaspora. Prerequisite: either AES 150, AFRAM 150, AFRAM 201, or POL S 201. Offered: jointly with POL S 315.

Course: AFRAM 318 - Black Literary Genres
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Considers how generic forms and conventions have been discussed and distributed in the larger context of African American, or other African diasporic literary studies. Links the relationship between generic forms to questions of power within social, cultural, and historical contexts. Offered: jointly with ENGL 318; AWSp.

Course: AFRAM 320 - Black Women in Drama
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Character types of black women as represented in plays by black women. Some black male playwrights are juxtaposed with black female writers for comparative analysis. Playwrights include Georgia Douglas Johnson, Angelina Grimke, Alice Chidress, Lorraine Hansberry, Ira Aldridge, LeRoi Jones.

Course: AFRAM 321 - History of African American Women and the Feminist Movement
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: "Feminist Movement" from early nineteenth century to present. Treats relationship between black and white women in their struggle for independence, at times together and at times apart. Discusses the reasons, process, and results of collaboration as well as opposition. Examines recent and contemporary attempts at cooperation. Offered: jointly with GWSS 321.

Course: AFRAM 330 - Music, Folklore, and Performance in Black Society
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Focuses on cultural expressions created by people of African descent in the Unites States in the twentieth century, with an emphasis on music, folklore, dance, and humor. Offered: WSp.

Course: AFRAM 332 - Black Sound Studies and the Archive
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Explores questions such as: What do official archives remember and forget? How does Black sound create a repository of history and memory where Black people are the makers and shapers of the story? What listening practices does Black sound elicit? Focuses on Black sound studies, including scholarship on race and the voice, music, soundscapes, audio technology, and listening to imagine alternative ways of creating, being, and belonging. Recommended: any AES, AAS, CHSTU, AFRAM, GWSS, or AIS course. Offered: AWSp.

Course: AFRAM 334 - Civil Rights and Black Power in the United States
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H/SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Examines the politics and culture of the modern African American freedom struggle, which began after WWII and continued into the 1970s. Interrogates political strategies associated with nonviolent direct action, armed self-reliance, and black nationalism, as well as the cultural expression that reflect these political currents. Offered: jointly with HSTAA 334.

Course: AFRAM 335 - Genre, Gender, and The Black Arts Movement
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Explores the literature, music, visual art, and performing arts of the Black Arts Movement, the sibling of the Black Power Movement. Students engage with in-depth reading assignments and sensory activities. Covers themes such as anti-Black violence, gender roles, self-love, socioeconomic inequity, and Black empowerment. Includes artists such as Larry Neal, Sonia Sanchez, Sun Ra, Katherine Dunham, and Barbara Jones-Hogu. Recommended: AFRAM 101; AFRAM 150/HSTAA 150; and AFRM 334/HSTAA 334.

Course: AFRAM 337 - Popular Music, Race, Identity, and Social Change
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc/A&H, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Focuses on popular music, shifting formations of race and identity and social change in various cultural, historical, and political contexts. Explores popular music as a tool for social change, a vehicle for community-building and a form of political and aesthetic expression. Course overlaps with: MUSIC 162 and BIS 363.

Course: AFRAM 340 - The Harlem Renaissance: A Literary Study
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Highlights Harlem Renaissance - 1912 through mid-1930s - as establishing a role for twentieth-century African American writer, encompassing literature, politics, and decolonization of the image of Africa, and solidifying integrationist and nationalist schools of thought. Examines images, themes, and characterizations in creating a literary aesthetic simultaneously American and African American.

Course: AFRAM 350 - Black Aesthetics
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H/SSc
Prerequisites: None
Description: Draws on both multi-media and print sources, including fiction, poetry, prose, films, polemics, historiography and speeches to explore the idea of a black aesthetic in various cultural, historical, and political contexts within the twentieth century.

Course: AFRAM 358 - African American Literature
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Selected writings, novels, short stories, plays, and poems by African American and African-descended writers in or from the United States. Study of the historical, cultural, and intellectual context for the development of literary work by such writers, including attention to identity, power, and inequality. Offered: jointly with ENGL 358.

Course: AFRAM 360 - Black Digital Studies
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Bridges and intersects two interdisciplinary fields - black studies and digital humanities. Attention to knowledge production.  Role of archives, collections, research centers, the black press, and digital technology. Ideas related to power, memory, resistance, perspective and respectability politics in storytelling and control of the vehicles used to do so. Recommended: introduction to sociology; and introductory courses in African American studies and communications.  Offered: AWSpS.

Course: AFRAM 370 - African American Political Thought
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc
Prerequisites: None
Description: Political ideologies and philosophies of pivotal African American historical figures and the conditions under which these ideologies are developed, rejected, and transformed. How ideologies relate to solution of African American political problems.

Course: AFRAM 380 - Health Disparities in Black Women
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Examines how social, economic, environmental, cultural, and lifestyle factors contribute to Black women's persistent and severe health disparities, as well as to disparities in their access to health care, quality of care received, and health insurance coverage. Recommended: any 100-level AFRAM, AES, AAS, or CHSTU course. Offered: AWSp.

Course: AFRAM 404 - Advanced African American Studies in Humanities
Credits: 5, max. 15
General Education: A&H, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Advanced and interdisciplinary engagement with racial formation, Black cultural production, and resistance among people of African descent throughout the Diaspora. Draws upon cultural studies perspectives with an emphasis on literature, film, music, performance, visual and material culture. Topics include art, labor, migration, politics; racial capitalism and political economy; social movements and cultural history; black intellectual traditions. Offered: AWSpS.

Course: AFRAM 405 - Advanced African American Studies in Social Science
Credits: 5, max. 15
General Education: SSc, DIV
Prerequisites: None
Description: Advanced study of racial formation, Black cultural production, and resistance among people of African descent throughout the Diaspora. Social science theories and methods used to examine various topics, including social scientific analysis of political history; social movements; intellectual traditions; theory; and intersections with urban, digital and legal studies; race, science, and biopolitics; public health and environmental studies. Offered: AWSpS.

Course: AFRAM 437 - Blacks in American Law
Credits: 5
General Education: SSc
Prerequisites: None
Description: Historical continuity for changing relationship between American jurisprudence and black Americans, 1640-1986. Statutory and case law which determined role of blacks in American society, and use of law by blacks to gain civil and personal rights.

Course: AFRAM 498 - Special Topics in African American Studies
Credits: 3-5, max. 15
General Education: SSc
Prerequisites: None
Description: Topics in which students and faculty have developed an interest as a result of work done in other classes or as a result of the need to investigate in greater depth Afro-American Studies issues. Topics vary.

Course: AFRAM 499 - Independent Study and Research
Credits: 1-5, max. 10
General Education: 
Prerequisites: None
Description: Identification and investigation of the problems and needs of the black community. Methods and alternatives of approaching these problems and needs. Students designate their areas of interest and subsequently pursue research and problem solving.
Office of the University Registrar
registrar@uw.edu
Modified: June 2, 2026

Course: ART 101 - Visual Art and Creative Thinking
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Explores how we observe/interpret our visual world. Introduces art and design terminology, techniques and analysis. Class sessions include short drawing, writing, critical thinking exercises, as well as lectures by visiting arts professionals and group activities. Ideal class for students exploring majors in Art, Art history or Design.

Course: ART 102 - Advanced Placement
Credits: AP
General Education: Studio Art: Drawing (5) A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Course awarded based on Advanced Placement (AP) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information.

Course: ART 103 - Advanced Placement
Credits: AP
General Education: Studio Art: 2D Design (5) A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Course awarded based on Advanced Placement (AP) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information.

Course: ART 104 - Advanced Placement
Credits: AP
General Education: Studio Art: 3D Design (5) A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Course awarded based on Advanced Placement (AP) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information.

Course: ART 105 - International Baccalaureate
Credits: IB
General Education: Visual Art (5) A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Course awarded based on International Baccalaureate (IB) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information.

Course: ART 131 - Alternative Approaches to Art
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Questions what art can be, where it can exist, and who it can engage. Investigates how artists across the world have historically challenged conventional methods, materials, and contexts. Students create projects that explore how art can connect ideas across disciplines. No prior art experience required.

Course: ART 140 - Basic Photography
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introduces theory, techniques, and processes of still photography in an artistic context. Emphasizes photography's creative potential. Course equivalent to: BIS 132.

Course: ART 141 - Introduction to Video
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introduces moving image media with a focus on independent and experimental film and video art. Students learn video production and post-production and are introduced to key moments and concepts in moving image history and criticism. Synthesizes approaches from contemporary video art, lens-based practices, and experimental cinema to develop both technical proficiency and critical thinking.

Course: ART 142 - Introduction to New Genres
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Develops abilities to generate ideas and translate concepts. Emphasis on questioning preconceived notions of the role of art in culture. History and practice of conceptually-based art, including installation, video, performance, and sound; challenges students to identify the appropriate form or medium to express ideas and content.

Course: ART 190 - Introduction to Drawing
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Builds basic drawing skills, develops understanding of primary concepts which relate to drawing and develops an understanding of the grammar or syntax of two-dimensional language. Students move beyond their current knowledge and abilities and link new skills, concepts, and understandings to creative expressing. Course equivalent to: BIS 121.

Course: ART 191 - Color Studies Studio
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Examination of color as a distinct visual phenomenon with investigations of its practical, theoretical, and illusionary aspects. Employs various media and materials in exercises and compositions that demonstrate properties of color structure, symbolism, and perception and the potential application to art and design.

Course: ART 200 - Artist Mindset
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Where do ideas come from? How does the practice of art connect across disciplines? Promotes critical curiosity.  Students gain experience evaluating images and developing ideas. Demystifies each individual's capacity to be an imaginative thinker, and dispels myths about what it means to be an artist today.

Course: ART 233 - Introduction to 3D4M: Glass
Credits: 5, max. 10
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: This introductory studio course investigates the material glass and its applications in the fine arts. Examines historical, technical, and contemporary practices. Prepares students for further exploration with the material at the intermediate and advanced levels.

Course: ART 240 - Intermediate Photomedia I
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: ART 140.
Description: Includes studio projects examining the expressive and conceptual uses of alternative photographic materials and techniques. May include and introduction to photography, large format, and studio lighting. Prerequisite: ART 140.

Course: ART 242 - Intermediate Photo, Media, and New Genres
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: either ART 140, ART 141, or ART 142.
Description: Expands foundational new genres concepts through intermediate-level projects emphasizing concept development and critical inquiry. Students investigate hybrid and experimental practices including performance, video, sound, installation, and emerging forms. Prerequisite: either ART 140, ART 141, or ART 142.

Course: ART 245 - Concepts in Printmaking
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introduction to contemporary printing methods such as monotype, monoprint, stencil, and photocopy. Survey of historical and current approaches to the art of printmaking.

Course: ART 246 - Works on Paper/Monotype
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introduces contemporary imaging methods, expands traditional drawing methods, and encourages relationship of content to structure. Introduces relationship of printmaking and painting to drawing through monotype methods.

Course: ART 253 - Introduction to 3D4M: Ceramics
Credits: 5, max. 10
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introduces ceramic hand-building and wheel throwing techniques. Explores functional and conceptual considerations with clay in a contemporary context and art practice. Offered: AWSp.

Course: ART 255 - Making Meaning: Art and Mathematics as Embodied Practices
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: What does it mean to create meaning in the intersection of two disciplines? Explore, via projects of making, a continuing dialogue between art and mathematics. Focusing inquiry on diverse global cultural traditions of embodied making, thinking, and imagining, students experiment with mathematical ideas, such as geometry, symmetries, and algorithmic processes, in a variety of media including photography, textiles, and movement.

Course: ART 260 - Introduction to Contemporary Art for Interdisciplinary Practice
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introduction to recurring themes and practices in the visual arts. Moves beyond medium-based categories, surveying a diverse range of issues that motivate artists and create content in contemporary art. Examines the importance and influence of the visual arts in the larger context of contemporary culture and society.

Course: ART 272 - Introduction to 3D4M: Sculpture
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: Introduces three dimensional concepts in the production of art and a critical, formal vocabulary in the pursuit of individual expression. Offers an overview of materials and technical processes.

Course: ART 281 - Art Makerspaces: Woodworking
Credits: 2
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: None
Description: The ideation process and creation of art and design require a strong knowledge of materials and equipment. Covers the knowledge and skill building in the medium of wood needed to turn concepts into reality. Recommended: No previous experience needed Credit/no-credit only.

Course: ART 290 - Beginning Drawing: Topics
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: ART 190. Offered: AWSp.
Description: Revolving topics in beginning level drawing. Explores fundamental elements of line, tone, form, and composition through various subjects and materials. Students explore concepts of perception and proportion through intense observation. Assignments and critiques develop experimentation and critical thinking. Prerequisite: ART 190. Offered: AWSp.

Course: ART 292 - Beginning Painting
Credits: 5, max. 10
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: ART 190.
Description: Beginning oil painting. Prerequisite: ART 190.

Course: ART 300 - Photography and Extended Practice
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: ART 240.
Description: Concepts and techniques of contemporary lens-based practices considered through discussions, image surveys, field trips, screenings, readings and critiques. Prerequisite: ART 240.

Course: ART 301 - Moving Images
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: ART 240.
Description: Development and articulation of an individual, experimental, and critical approach to artistic studio practice using the moving image. Prerequisite: ART 240.

Course: ART 302 - Research and Exhibition Practice
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: ART 240.
Description: Students expand their practice through experimentation with a variety of materials and presentation strategies. Completed work exhibited in spaces across the UW. One-on-one and group feedback. Prerequisite: ART 240.

Course: ART 303 - Aesthetics of the Everyday
Credits: 5
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: ART 240.
Description: Studio-based course. Utilizing social media, video, and critique skills, students learn the importance of art-making in today's culture. Introduction to installation and performance art. Investigates contemporary contexts in which art is produced and presented. Relies on philosophical teachings and artistic practices of everyday life as a guide. Prerequisite: ART 240.

Course: ART 333 - New Materials and Processes in Glass
Credits: 5, max. 15
General Education: A&H
Prerequisites: ART 233.
Description: Explores the process through which artists discover and translate ideas, feelings, and concerns into images or objects. Investigations include various glass forming methodologies and material properties. Topics rotate and may include mold-making, kiln casting, photo processes, mixed medial, cold fabrication, enamels, cold-working, hot glass practices, and sculptural applications. Prerequisite: ART 233.

ground_truths: AFRAM 101 (Introduction to African American Studies) covers: History, culture, religion, institutions, politics, economics, arts, and psychology of peoples of African descent as developed from experience in both the old and new worlds. Multidisciplinary analysis of social life from a black perspective as illustrated in selected historical and contemporary writings. Prerequisites: None.
AFRAM 102 (Advanced Placement) covers: Course awarded based on Advanced Placement (AP) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 150 (Introduction to African American History) covers: Introductory survey of topics and problems in African American history with some attention to Africa as well as to America. Basic introductory course for sequence of lecture courses and seminars in African American history. Offered: jointly with HSTAA 150. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 214 (Introduction African American Literature) covers: Introduction to various genres of African American literature from its beginnings to the present. Emphasizes the cultural and historical context of African American literary expression and its aesthetics criteria. Explores key issues and debates, such as race and racism, inequality, literary form, and canonical acceptance. Offered: jointly with ENGL 258. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 220 (African American Film Studies) covers: Examines the history and theory of African American filmmaking, introducing central political and aesthetic debates by way of different cinematic eras, genres, and filmmakers. Focuses primarily on black directors and producers independent and commercial contexts as they confront popular representations of U.S. blackness in their own cinematic practice. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 241 (The United States During the Era of Civil War and Reconstruction) covers: Views American Civil War and Reconstruction through lens of African-American social history. From U.S. government's abolition of Atlantic slave trade in 1808 to expanding Jim Crow laws in 1890s, reports how changes in the era were racialized and gendered. Considers how ideas of race, gender, class, and freedom in the U.S. were challenged and reshaped. Explores historical legacies of Civil War and Reconstruction from the nineteenth-century to now. Offered: jointly with HSTAA 241. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 246 (African American Politics) covers: Survey of African Americans within the U.S. socio-political processes. Situates African Americans within a post-civil rights context where there is debate about race's centrality to an African American politics. Offered: jointly with POL S 246. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 260 (African American Family) covers: Explores cultural processes as well as political, economic, and social structures and policies that have shaped, and continue to shape, the ways in which African American families have formed and functioned. Examines the history, experiences, struggles, and progress of African American families in the U.S. beginning with pre-Colonial Africa and ending with current issues facing African American families. Offered: jointly with SOC 260. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 261 (The African American Experience through Literature) covers: Instructs students in hermeneutical and sociological methods of analyses. Analyzes selected novels, essays, poems, short stories, and plays with the purpose of understanding the structures and functions of both society and personality. Offered: jointly with SOC 261. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 270 (The Jazz Age) covers: Interdisciplinary study of period after World War I to Great Crash. African American and Anglo American currents and impulses that flowed together in the Roaring Twenties. Covers politics of normalcy, economics of margin, literature of indulgence and confusion, transformation of race relations, and cultural influence of jazz. Offered: jointly with HSTAA 270. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 272 (History of the South Since the Civil War) covers: Reconstruction and its aftermath, the Agrarian (Populist) revolt, disfranchisement and segregation, the effects of urbanization and subsequent depression, desegregation, and the struggle for civil rights. Examines the New South, the conflict of ideology with structural and material change, and the place of the South in contemporary America. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 315 (Black Identities and Political Power) covers: Relates the deployment of political power within institutions to shifting racial identities. Shows how racial identities both reflect and inflect relations of domination and resistance within and between cultures in the black diaspora. Prerequisite: either AES 150, AFRAM 150, AFRAM 201, or POL S 201. Offered: jointly with POL S 315. Prerequisites: either AES 150, AFRAM 150, AFRAM 201, or POL S 201. Offered: jointly with POL S 315..

AFRAM 318 (Black Literary Genres) covers: Considers how generic forms and conventions have been discussed and distributed in the larger context of African American, or other African diasporic literary studies. Links the relationship between generic forms to questions of power within social, cultural, and historical contexts. Offered: jointly with ENGL 318; AWSp. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 320 (Black Women in Drama) covers: Character types of black women as represented in plays by black women. Some black male playwrights are juxtaposed with black female writers for comparative analysis. Playwrights include Georgia Douglas Johnson, Angelina Grimke, Alice Chidress, Lorraine Hansberry, Ira Aldridge, LeRoi Jones. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 321 (History of African American Women and the Feminist Movement) covers: "Feminist Movement" from early nineteenth century to present. Treats relationship between black and white women in their struggle for independence, at times together and at times apart. Discusses the reasons, process, and results of collaboration as well as opposition. Examines recent and contemporary attempts at cooperation. Offered: jointly with GWSS 321. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 330 (Music, Folklore, and Performance in Black Society) covers: Focuses on cultural expressions created by people of African descent in the Unites States in the twentieth century, with an emphasis on music, folklore, dance, and humor. Offered: WSp. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 332 (Black Sound Studies and the Archive) covers: Explores questions such as: What do official archives remember and forget? How does Black sound create a repository of history and memory where Black people are the makers and shapers of the story? What listening practices does Black sound elicit? Focuses on Black sound studies, including scholarship on race and the voice, music, soundscapes, audio technology, and listening to imagine alternative ways of creating, being, and belonging. Recommended: any AES, AAS, CHSTU, AFRAM, GWSS, or AIS course. Offered: AWSp. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 334 (Civil Rights and Black Power in the United States) covers: Examines the politics and culture of the modern African American freedom struggle, which began after WWII and continued into the 1970s. Interrogates political strategies associated with nonviolent direct action, armed self-reliance, and black nationalism, as well as the cultural expression that reflect these political currents. Offered: jointly with HSTAA 334. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 335 (Genre, Gender, and The Black Arts Movement) covers: Explores the literature, music, visual art, and performing arts of the Black Arts Movement, the sibling of the Black Power Movement. Students engage with in-depth reading assignments and sensory activities. Covers themes such as anti-Black violence, gender roles, self-love, socioeconomic inequity, and Black empowerment. Includes artists such as Larry Neal, Sonia Sanchez, Sun Ra, Katherine Dunham, and Barbara Jones-Hogu. Recommended: AFRAM 101; AFRAM 150/HSTAA 150; and AFRM 334/HSTAA 334. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 337 (Popular Music, Race, Identity, and Social Change) covers: Focuses on popular music, shifting formations of race and identity and social change in various cultural, historical, and political contexts. Explores popular music as a tool for social change, a vehicle for community-building and a form of political and aesthetic expression. Course overlaps with: MUSIC 162 and BIS 363. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 340 (The Harlem Renaissance: A Literary Study) covers: Highlights Harlem Renaissance - 1912 through mid-1930s - as establishing a role for twentieth-century African American writer, encompassing literature, politics, and decolonization of the image of Africa, and solidifying integrationist and nationalist schools of thought. Examines images, themes, and characterizations in creating a literary aesthetic simultaneously American and African American. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 350 (Black Aesthetics) covers: Draws on both multi-media and print sources, including fiction, poetry, prose, films, polemics, historiography and speeches to explore the idea of a black aesthetic in various cultural, historical, and political contexts within the twentieth century. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 358 (African American Literature) covers: Selected writings, novels, short stories, plays, and poems by African American and African-descended writers in or from the United States. Study of the historical, cultural, and intellectual context for the development of literary work by such writers, including attention to identity, power, and inequality. Offered: jointly with ENGL 358. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 360 (Black Digital Studies) covers: Bridges and intersects two interdisciplinary fields - black studies and digital humanities. Attention to knowledge production.  Role of archives, collections, research centers, the black press, and digital technology. Ideas related to power, memory, resistance, perspective and respectability politics in storytelling and control of the vehicles used to do so. Recommended: introduction to sociology; and introductory courses in African American studies and communications.  Offered: AWSpS. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 370 (African American Political Thought) covers: Political ideologies and philosophies of pivotal African American historical figures and the conditions under which these ideologies are developed, rejected, and transformed. How ideologies relate to solution of African American political problems. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 380 (Health Disparities in Black Women) covers: Examines how social, economic, environmental, cultural, and lifestyle factors contribute to Black women's persistent and severe health disparities, as well as to disparities in their access to health care, quality of care received, and health insurance coverage. Recommended: any 100-level AFRAM, AES, AAS, or CHSTU course. Offered: AWSp. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 404 (Advanced African American Studies in Humanities) covers: Advanced and interdisciplinary engagement with racial formation, Black cultural production, and resistance among people of African descent throughout the Diaspora. Draws upon cultural studies perspectives with an emphasis on literature, film, music, performance, visual and material culture. Topics include art, labor, migration, politics; racial capitalism and political economy; social movements and cultural history; black intellectual traditions. Offered: AWSpS. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 405 (Advanced African American Studies in Social Science) covers: Advanced study of racial formation, Black cultural production, and resistance among people of African descent throughout the Diaspora. Social science theories and methods used to examine various topics, including social scientific analysis of political history; social movements; intellectual traditions; theory; and intersections with urban, digital and legal studies; race, science, and biopolitics; public health and environmental studies. Offered: AWSpS. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 437 (Blacks in American Law) covers: Historical continuity for changing relationship between American jurisprudence and black Americans, 1640-1986. Statutory and case law which determined role of blacks in American society, and use of law by blacks to gain civil and personal rights. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 498 (Special Topics in African American Studies) covers: Topics in which students and faculty have developed an interest as a result of work done in other classes or as a result of the need to investigate in greater depth Afro-American Studies issues. Topics vary. Prerequisites: None.

AFRAM 499 (Independent Study and Research) covers: Identification and investigation of the problems and needs of the black community. Methods and alternatives of approaching these problems and needs. Students designate their areas of interest and subsequently pursue research and problem solving.

Modified: June 2, 2026 Prerequisites: None.

ART 101 (Visual Art and Creative Thinking) covers: Explores how we observe/interpret our visual world. Introduces art and design terminology, techniques and analysis. Class sessions include short drawing, writing, critical thinking exercises, as well as lectures by visiting arts professionals and group activities. Ideal class for students exploring majors in Art, Art history or Design. Prerequisites: None.
ART 102 (Advanced Placement) covers: Course awarded based on Advanced Placement (AP) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information. Prerequisites: None.
ART 103 (Advanced Placement) covers: Course awarded based on Advanced Placement (AP) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information. Prerequisites: None.
ART 104 (Advanced Placement) covers: Course awarded based on Advanced Placement (AP) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information. Prerequisites: None.

ART 105 (International Baccalaureate) covers: Course awarded based on International Baccalaureate (IB) score. Consult the Admissions Exams for Credit website for more information. Prerequisites: None.

ART 131 (Alternative Approaches to Art) covers: Questions what art can be, where it can exist, and who it can engage. Investigates how artists across the world have historically challenged conventional methods, materials, and contexts. Students create projects that explore how art can connect ideas across disciplines. No prior art experience required. Prerequisites: None.

ART 140 (Basic Photography) covers: Introduces theory, techniques, and processes of still photography in an artistic context. Emphasizes photography's creative potential. Course equivalent to: BIS 132. Prerequisites: None.

ART 141 (Introduction to Video) covers: Introduces moving image media with a focus on independent and experimental film and video art. Students learn video production and post-production and are introduced to key moments and concepts in moving image history and criticism. Synthesizes approaches from contemporary video art, lens-based practices, and experimental cinema to develop both technical proficiency and critical thinking. Prerequisites: None.

ART 142 (Introduction to New Genres) covers: Develops abilities to generate ideas and translate concepts. Emphasis on questioning preconceived notions of the role of art in culture. History and practice of conceptually-based art, including installation, video, performance, and sound; challenges students to identify the appropriate form or medium to express ideas and content. Prerequisites: None.

ART 190 (Introduction to Drawing) covers: Builds basic drawing skills, develops understanding of primary concepts which relate to drawing and develops an understanding of the grammar or syntax of two-dimensional language. Students move beyond their current knowledge and abilities and link new skills, concepts, and understandings to creative expressing. Course equivalent to: BIS 121. Prerequisites: None.

ART 191 (Color Studies Studio) covers: Examination of color as a distinct visual phenomenon with investigations of its practical, theoretical, and illusionary aspects. Employs various media and materials in exercises and compositions that demonstrate properties of color structure, symbolism, and perception and the potential application to art and design. Prerequisites: None.

ART 200 (Artist Mindset) covers: Where do ideas come from? How does the practice of art connect across disciplines? Promotes critical curiosity.  Students gain experience evaluating images and developing ideas. Demystifies each individual's capacity to be an imaginative thinker, and dispels myths about what it means to be an artist today. Prerequisites: None.

ART 233 (Introduction to 3D4M: Glass) covers: This introductory studio course investigates the material glass and its applications in the fine arts. Examines historical, technical, and contemporary practices. Prepares students for further exploration with the material at the intermediate and advanced levels. Prerequisites: None.

ART 240 (Intermediate Photomedia I) covers: Includes studio projects examining the expressive and conceptual uses of alternative photographic materials and techniques. May include and introduction to photography, large format, and studio lighting. Prerequisite: ART 140. Prerequisites: ART 140..

ART 242 (Intermediate Photo, Media, and New Genres) covers: Expands foundational new genres concepts through intermediate-level projects emphasizing concept development and critical inquiry. Students investigate hybrid and experimental practices including performance, video, sound, installation, and emerging forms. Prerequisite: either ART 140, ART 141, or ART 142. Prerequisites: either ART 140, ART 141, or ART 142..

ART 245 (Concepts in Printmaking) covers: Introduction to contemporary printing methods such as monotype, monoprint, stencil, and photocopy. Survey of historical and current approaches to the art of printmaking. Prerequisites: None.

ART 246 (Works on Paper/Monotype) covers: Introduces contemporary imaging methods, expands traditional drawing methods, and encourages relationship of content to structure. Introduces relationship of printmaking and painting to drawing through monotype methods. Prerequisites: None.

ART 253 (Introduction to 3D4M: Ceramics) covers: Introduces ceramic hand-building and wheel throwing techniques. Explores functional and conceptual considerations with clay in a contemporary context and art practice. Offered: AWSp. Prerequisites: None.

ART 255 (Making Meaning: Art and Mathematics as Embodied Practices) covers: What does it mean to create meaning in the intersection of two disciplines? Explore, via projects of making, a continuing dialogue between art and mathematics. Focusing inquiry on diverse global cultural traditions of embodied making, thinking, and imagining, students experiment with mathematical ideas, such as geometry, symmetries, and algorithmic processes, in a variety of media including photography, textiles, and movement. Prerequisites: None.

ART 260 (Introduction to Contemporary Art for Interdisciplinary Practice) covers: Introduction to recurring themes and practices in the visual arts. Moves beyond medium-based categories, surveying a diverse range of issues that motivate artists and create content in contemporary art. Examines the importance and influence of the visual arts in the larger context of contemporary culture and society. Prerequisites: None.

ART 272 (Introduction to 3D4M: Sculpture) covers: Introduces three dimensional concepts in the production of art and a critical, formal vocabulary in the pursuit of individual expression. Offers an overview of materials and technical processes. Prerequisites: None.

ART 281 (Art Makerspaces: Woodworking) covers: The ideation process and creation of art and design require a strong knowledge of materials and equipment. Covers the knowledge and skill building in the medium of wood needed to turn concepts into reality. Recommended: No previous experience needed Credit/no-credit only. Prerequisites: None.

ART 290 (Beginning Drawing: Topics) covers: Revolving topics in beginning level drawing. Explores fundamental elements of line, tone, form, and composition through various subjects and materials. Students explore concepts of perception and proportion through intense observation. Assignments and critiques develop experimentation and critical thinking. Prerequisite: ART 190. Offered: AWSp. Prerequisites: ART 190. Offered: AWSp..
ART 292 (Beginning Painting) covers: Beginning oil painting. Prerequisite: ART 190. Prerequisites: ART 190..

ART 300 (Photography and Extended Practice) covers: Concepts and techniques of contemporary lens-based practices considered through discussions, image surveys, field trips, screenings, readings and critiques. Prerequisite: ART 240. Prerequisites: ART 240..

ART 301 (Moving Images) covers: Development and articulation of an individual, experimental, and critical approach to artistic studio practice using the moving image. Prerequisite: ART 240. Prerequisites: ART 240..

ART 302 (Research and Exhibition Practice) covers: Students expand their practice through experimentation with a variety of materials and presentation strategies. Completed work exhibited in spaces across the UW. One-on-one and group feedback. Prerequisite: ART 240. Prerequisites: ART 240..

ART 303 (Aesthetics of the Everyday) covers: Studio-based course. Utilizing social media, video, and critique skills, students learn the importance of art-making in today's culture. Introduction to installation and performance art. Investigates contemporary contexts in which art is produced and presented. Relies on philosophical teachings and artistic practices of everyday life as a guide. Prerequisite: ART 240. Prerequisites: ART 240..

ART 333 (New Materials and Processes in Glass) covers: Explores the process through which artists discover and translate ideas, feelings, and concerns into images or objects. Investigations include various glass forming methodologies and material properties. Topics rotate and may include mold-making, kiln casting, photo processes, mixed medial, cold fabrication, enamels, cold-working, hot glass practices, and sculptural applications. Prerequisite: ART 233. Prerequisites: ART 233..
